content.text: Ṣaḥīḥ Muslim is a collection of hadīth compiled by Imām Muslim ibn al-Hajjāj al-Naysāburi (rahimahullāh). His collection is considered to be one of the most authentic collections of the Sunnah of the Prophet (ﷺ), and along with Ṣaḥīḥ al-Bukhārī forms the 'Sahihain,' or the 'Two Sahihs.' It contains roughly 7,500 hadīth (with repetitions) in 57 books.
The translation provided here is by Abdul Hamid Siddiqui.

Imām Muslim's full name is Abu al-Husayn Muslim ibn al-Hajjāj ibn Muslim ibn Warat al-Qushayri al-Naysaburi (206–261 AH / 821–875 AD). He belonged to the Qushayr tribe, an offshoot of the great Arab clan of Rabi'a.

He was born in Naysabur (Nishapur) in 206 AH / 821 AD. He travelled extensively throughout Arabia, Egypt, Syria and Iraq in search of hadith, studying under renowned scholars such as Isḥāq ibn Rāḥawayh, Aḥmad ibn Hanbal, 'Ubaydullah al-Qawariri, Qutaiba ibn Sa’id, 'Abdullah ibn Maslama, Harmalah ibn Yahya and many others.

After completing his education, he settled in Nishapur where he became closely associated with Imām al-Bukhārī. He remained one of his most devoted students and supporters, even during the disagreement between Imām al-Bukhārī and Muhammad ibn Yahya al-Dhuhali regarding the issue of the creation of the Qur'an.

Imām Muslim authored numerous works on Hadith, the most famous being his Ṣaḥīḥ, originally titled 'Musnad as-Ṣaḥīḥ.' He explained that he compiled it in response to a request from one of his students.

He carefully examined approximately 300,000 hadith and selected around 4,000 authentic narrations after rigorous scrutiny. He also authored several other important works including Kitab al-Musnad al-Kabir 'Ala al-Rijal, Jami' Kabir, Kitab al-Asma' wa'l-Kuna, Kitab al-Ilal and Kitab al-Wijdan.

Imam Muslim strictly adhered to the principles of Hadith science and accepted only narrations transmitted through an unbroken chain of trustworthy narrators up to the Prophet (ﷺ), while ensuring harmony with other authentic reports.

items: Narrators of perfect memory, integrity and trustworthiness.
Narrators with slightly lesser precision but still reliable, knowledgeable and truthful.
Narrators whose honesty was disputed or questionable; Imam Muslim did not rely on such narrators.

content.text: Imam Muslim carefully preserved the exact wording of narrations, distinguished between 'haddathana' and 'akhbarana', and ensured continuity in every chain of narration by requiring multiple reliable transmitters throughout the chain.
Ṣaḥīḥ Muslim was later explained by Imām an-Nawawi and one of his teachers, Abu 'Amr ibn Salah.
His notable students include Abu Hatim Razi, Musa ibn Harun, Ahmad ibn Salama, Abu 'Isa Tirmidhi, Abu Bakr ibn Khusaima, Abu 'Awana and Al-Dhahabi.
Imam Muslim lived for fifty-five years. He devoted his life to learning, compiling, teaching and transmitting Hadith. He passed away in 261 AH / 875 AD and was buried in the outskirts of Nishapur.
